entry.title: Public Cloud: Depreciation Key for Half Year Convention in Chart of Depreciation "IFRS"
entry.content:
Dear Experts, 

I have the following problem:

We have the depreciation Key “LINH” in Chart of Depreciation “3910”. Here the period control method is set to “006” by SAP standard. Behind that period control method, there are several values that control at what time the system starts to calculate the depreciation of the asset.. This is what our customer needs for local accounting standards. We want to use IFRS as a tax ledger, as we cannot use the extension ledger due to lack of integration with asset accounting. 

Above you can see a screenshot of “LINH”. We see Period Control Method “006”. Under the column “Acq” there is the value 04, which stands for First year convention at half year start date:


This is exactly what we need in the IFRS Chart of Depreciation. So far so good.

Now, the period control method “006” in Chart of Depreciation “IFRS” differs from “006” in “3910”. 
The following screenshot is “006” in “IFRS”: 



As you can see, under column “Acq”, the value is 01, which stands for pro rata at period start date. This means Period Control Method 006 from 3910 is NOT the same as period control method 006 in IFRS. This is the real problem, as the system does not allow us to open the value help of the period control method, to see which method has 04 in acq. We do not have any option to look up, what period control method in Chart of Depreciation “IFRS” has the correct values in the columns to the right. That is also why we cannot fill out the template provided by SAP Support as we do not know what the period control method is controlling in the background. Additionally, we received the following screenshot from the support, which values are possible: 

As we can see, there is no value that has 04 in Aqc Column. Does anybody know any workaround in this regard? I would really appreciate any kind of advice,

Best regards
Elen

entry.title: A Rundown of API Lifecycle in SAP BTP: End - End tutorial for beginners
entry.content:
Introduction: In this weblog, I'll give you a rundown of API lifecycle in SAP BTP - beginning with how to create an API Provider, API Proxy, testing a Product through the Developer Portal.

(I) Create API Provider: For creating an API provider first go to Configure>>APIs>>API Providers>>Click on Create>>Give the details like name, host, authentication>> then save it and test the connection.

(II) Create API Proxy:  Select the API Provider which you have created above then select the service from the available list for your Api Provider.

Specify the Proxy Details: name>title>>base path>>version>> click on create and review the target endpoint and resources>>then save it>>Deploy it for activation of your API proxy.

(III)  In this step we need to add policies click on the policies>>edit>>flows>>TargetEndpoint>>PostFlow

Here, I have added two policies one is Assign Message Policy and another is Basic Authentication.

In Assign message policy- 

(i)add name 

(ii) the XML, editor give your username and password  

 

 

Now you have to add Basic Authentication Policy here you have to add the name and check these details like Endpoint type, flow type and stream.

Then Click on update>>save>>redeploy it again.

(IV) For Creating a Product - Engage>>Product>>Create

Then move to APIs tab press on add button>> now choose the API which you have created earlier>>click on OK button and then publish it

You will see there will be an entry in APIs column with the status of Published.

Click on Explore our Ecosystem >> Developer Hub

Now you can see there is a product. 

(V) Test the Product: Select the Product>> ProductSet >> GET /ProductSet >>Try it out >>Execute

Then it will give the response of Product Set with a HTTP status code 200.

entry.title: Re: SAP Datasphere dynamic source filter
entry.content:
Hi - it's a Dataflow. I tried this  TO_NVARCHAR(TO_TIMESTAMP($MAX_VALUE_LastModifiedDate), 'YYYY-MM-DD"T"HH24:MI:SS'), however I get this....

Invalid column name "TO_NVARCHAR(TO_TIMESTAMP(LastModifiedDate), 'YYYY-MM-DD"T"HH24:MI:SS')" entered in the filter expression.

I don't think Open Connectors sources are allowed for Replication flows.

The $MAX_VALUE_LastModifiedDate works as expected but I cant use a modifier around this unfortunately. Thanks for the suggestion though.

entry.title: Re: WITH HINT is highly needed in SAP CAP

entry.title: Re: Configuration: SAP Ariba SSO with SAP Cloud Identity Services - Identity Authentication
entry.content:
This blog has been updated to reflect:

- SAP Ariba SAML Authentication Self-service Configuration (PLICM-871) is now OBSOLETE for SAP Ariba SSO setup with SAP Cloud Identity Services (CIS) and replaced by automation - see Configure Authentication with SAP Cloud Identity Services for SAP Ariba Solutions in Intelligent Configuration Manager (CFG-324). For SAP Ariba SSO setup with any other Corporate IdP beside SAP Cloud Integration Services (CIS) the SAP Ariba SAML Authentication Self-service Configuration (PLICM-871) is still valid.

- PS-1444 Feature Toggle usage to switch the SAP Ariba SSO SAML 2.0 Issuer from http to https

- PLAF-1869 Feature Toggle usage to turn the SAP Ariba SSO for SAP Ariba Third-Party users

SAP Ariba Procurement, cloud edition SAP Ariba Sourcing, cloud edition SAP Cloud Identity Services

entry.title: From Legacy to Leading-Edge: Streamlining Telecommunications IT with SAP LeanIX
entry.content:
Introduction to the Application Rationalization Use Case in SAP LeanIX

In this blog, we will provide a comprehensive, step-by-step guide to application rationalization using the SAP LeanIX platform. I will begin by outlining the strategic importance and benefits of optimizing an application portfolio, such as cost reduction and risk mitigation. I will then detail the process for a Telecommunications company called MapleConnect Communications, from understanding business objectives and scoping relevant applications to collecting and evaluating data on functional and technical fit. Finally, I will explain how to create a transformation roadmap, initiate planned changes, and track and report progress within SAP LeanIX, emphasizing the platform's features for data management, analysis, and visualization.

This entire use case was set up in my own Workspace for this Telecommunications company from scratch. In the context of the Application Rationalization use case, several SAP LeanIX Fact Sheet types are utilized to build a comprehensive data repository and manage the rationalization process. Here are the LeanIX Fact Sheets involved in this use case:

  • Business Capability Fact Sheets: Applications can be grouped or filtered by business capability domains. Their relations to applications are part of the baseline information in the inventory that is assumed to exist at the beginning of the "Enrich Data" step. You can narrow down the scope of applications for assessment to a specific business capability domain, such as 'Finance', by filtering on these Fact Sheets.
  • Application Fact Sheets: These are the central focus of the application rationalization assessment. They hold critical information such as lifecycle, business criticality, hosting type, and basic relations to business capabilities and IT components. During the process, they are enriched with data on functional fit, technical fit, and cost in relation to an application and IT components. Decisions regarding TIME classification (Tolerate, Invest, Migrate, Eliminate) are also recorded for these Fact Sheets.
  • Objective Fact Sheets: These are used to define major strategy cornerstones and targets for the Application Rationalization initiative. They can be linked with Parent/Child relations to show how different objectives work together. Projects created as part of the roadmap are linked to Objective Fact Sheets to outline how they support the strategy.
  • Project/Initiative Fact Sheets: These are essential for visualizing roadmaps and planning application rationalization initiatives. A main "Application Rationalization Initiative" Project Fact Sheet is created, with child Project Fact Sheets for each TIME classification (Tolerate, Invest, Migrate, Eliminate.) A third hierarchy level can be created for each wave within these classifications. Applications are linked to their respective wave projects. These can also be used to model current or future changes to an application's architecture.
  • IT Component Fact Sheets: These Fact Sheets relate to applications and store lifecycle data. Cost information can be associated with the relation between an application and its IT components. Changes to IT components are documented when transforming applications.
  • Organization Fact Sheets: It exists as part of the broader SAP LeanIX meta-model and has associated modelling guidelines. The detailed steps for the Application Rationalization use case do not explicitly outline their direct use or interaction in gathering, enriching, evaluating, or reporting on applications, unlike other Fact Sheet types such as Application, Objective, Project, IT Component, and Business Capability Fact Sheets. While understanding organizational scope and stakeholders is crucial for the initiative, the official documentation does not specify using Organization Fact Sheets to record or leverage this information within the documented workflow for Application Rationalization. An Organization Fact Sheet can represent a Legal Entity, Region, Business Unit, Team, or Customer.

In summary, Application Rationalization is a strategic initiative aimed at streamlining an organization's existing application portfolio to enhance efficiency, reduce complexity, foster innovation, and lower the Total Cost of Ownership (TCO). The SAP LeanIX platform offers a structured, step-by-step approach to achieve these goals, enabling organizations to gain a "360° view of their application landscape" and identify rationalization candidates efficiently. Through a typical project duration of "six to 10 weeks from start to implementation," SAP LeanIX facilitates significant cost savings, redundancy elimination, and risk mitigation.

Based on SAP LeanIX customer experiences and research, some of the tangible benefits realized by Application Rationalization:

  • "Achieve $50M savings with application portfolio rationalization."
  • "Remove 30% of redundant applications and IT components within six months."
  • "Free up to $1.3M financial resource for innovation."
  • "Save $1.2M in application costs per year."
  • "Save up to $500K in two weeks by decommissioning 10 unused apps."
  • "80% of SAP LeanIX survey respondents plan to carry out application rationalization in 2023."

MapleConnect Communications Background

MapleConnect Communications was founded in 1880 as the Canadian Telephone Company, expanding rapidly through mergers and pioneering long‑distance service across the young Confederation. By the 1920s, under its new banner, Bell Communications Enterprises, it had built itself into Canada’s telephony backbone and, later, a North American pioneer in digital data and wireless services. Today, MapleConnect mirrors that heritage: serving 30 million customers from Vancouver to Veracruz, it offers voice, data, television, and IoT products across Canada and the U.S.

Despite its market leadership, MapleConnect’s IT estate is a patchwork of best‑of‑breed on‑premises systems, ranging from SAP ERP 6.0 modules and SAP APO to dozens of niche SaaS tools that have grown unwieldy and brittle.

Step 1: Understand Our IT and Business Strategy

Before we dive into rationalizing MapleConnect Communications’ sprawling application landscape, we must first ground ourselves in the company’s strategic objectives and current IT realities. As Canada’s premier telecommunications provider, serving 30 million voice, data, and wireless customers across Canada and the U.S., MapleConnect is laser-focused on the following strategic objectives:

  • Delivering a Superior Customer Experience: Legacy point‑to‑point integrations and manual processes introduce delays and errors across billing, order management, and support, undermining brand loyalty at every touchpoint. Therefore, we at MapleConnect need to reduce friction at every touchpoint (billing, support, provisioning) to drive loyalty and reduce churn.
  • Ambitious Growth Targets: To hit $200 billion USD in revenue and $60 ARPU within two years, MapleConnect needs scalable, cloud‑native platforms, not sprawling on‑prem silos. Therefore, we need to scale revenue to $200 billion USD and boost ARPU to $60 within two years by leveraging new 5G-enabled services.
  • Churn Reduction: With 5 percent annual churn (costing millions) and rising operational expenses, the executive team has mandated a 20 percent reduction in process costs over three years. Therefore, we need to tighten service-quality SLAs to cut annual customer attrition from 5 percent to 2 percent within 12 months.
  • Operational Digitization & Cost Efficiency: Last year’s 18‑hour network failure, rooted in a legacy ESB integration breach, cost $10.5 million in revenue, fines, and reputational damage. Our goal is to digitize core processes and drive a 20 percent reduction in operating expenses over the next three years.

These strategic objectives are represented in SAP LeanIX with the Objective fact sheet.

Last year’s 18-hour network outage, triggered by a legacy ESB’s incompatibility with aging on-prem CRM, billing, and provisioning systems, rattled executive leadership. That single event cost over $10 million in lost revenue, $2.5 million in regulatory fines, and inflicted significant reputational damage. Key root causes included:

  • Fragmented, Best-of-Breed IT Stack: Dozens of on-premises point solutions (from SAP ERP 6.0 EHP8 to Zuora CPQ & Billing to Salesforce Service Cloud) connected via a single legacy ESB, creating multiple single points of failure.
  • Insufficient Change Management & Documentation: Patching one component cascaded into unexpected downstream incompatibilities.
  • Weak Disaster-Recovery Processes: Manual failover planning and untested runbooks delayed recovery by hours.

MapleConnect's leadership has therefore mandated a “cloud-first, suite-first” posture: migrate away from on-prem SAP ECC, retire isolated point tools, and standardize on SAP S/4HANA Cloud and SAP BTP. This mandate guides our entire applications rationalization initiative, leveraging SAP LeanIX capabilities in this area.

The following are the key steps in understanding MapleConnect's strategy:

  1. Review MapleConnect IT and business strategy documents.
  2. Understand what success means for MapleConnect.
  3. Define MapleConnect major strategy cornerstones and targets of their initiative.
  4. Identify relevant stakeholders.
  5. Cluster MapleConnect applications using TIME framework (Tolerate, Invest, Migrate, Eliminate).

Step 2: Scope Applications

The goal in this step is to scope applications to a 'digestive' subset to help us gain experiences and achieve benefits early. With strategy clarified, we defined which applications to include in our rationalization effort. Our priority is on critical, revenue-driving systems that underpin customer experience, network operations, and financial management. We identified six flagship legacy applications (plus their supporting middleware) for deep assessment:

  1. SAP ERP 6.0 EHP8 (FICO, SD, PP): Live on-premises since 2012, handling core financial accounting, order-to-cash, and production planning for network rollout.
  2. SAP APO 7.13: On-premises Advanced Planning and Optimization used for forecasting capacity, inventory planning of network equipment, and demand planning for upstream suppliers.
  3. Zuora CPQ & Billing: SaaS-based billing engine used externally for customer invoice generation, rating, and subscription management; integrated via custom .NET connectors.
  4. Adobe Experience Manager (AEM): On-premises CMS for customer self-service portals, coupled with Adobe Target and Adobe Journey Optimizer for campaign management.
  5. Salesforce Service Cloud: Cloud-hosted CRM for customer support ticketing, field-service dispatch, and case management, integrated via a legacy ESB to SAP ERP.
  6. Legacy ESB / Custom Middleware: A homegrown enterprise service bus built in 2008 that glues together SAP ERP, Zuora, AEM, Salesforce, and several in-house provisioning tools.

For each application, we assigned a Business Owner (e.g., VP Customer Experience, VP Finance, VP Network Operations) and an IT Owner (e.g., ERP Team Lead, Integration Architect). This ensures accountability and granular input as we gather detailed information.

Part of scoping our applications landscape, we needed to collect information about existing  applications, and the following methods were followed:

  • Automated Scans: Connect LeanIX’s landscape discovery for cloud and on‑prem services and systems like SAP ERP 6.0 EHP8 (FICO, SD, PP), SAP APO 7.13, IBM Sterling Track & Trace, and legacy OSS/BSS.
  • Stakeholder Interviews: Engage VP Network Operations, IT Service Managers, and Process Owners to surface grassroots solutions, e.g., Accela Civic Platform for permits, Anaplan for planning, and myriad Adobe Experience Cloud tools.
  • Architecture Workshops: Map data flows between Auth0/Okta MFA, custom billing engines (Zuora CPQ & Billing), and middleware (legacy ESB), capturing dependencies for all 100+ tools in scope.

Step 3: Enrich Data

Now, we have collected rich, quantitative and qualitative data for each scoped application. Our objective was to go beyond simple “what-is-it” inventories and capture metrics around operational health, functional fit, cost, usage, and risk. This is by far, is the most time-consuming part of the Application Rationalization data gathering exercise. Surveys are the most effective method of gathering this data and updating the fact sheets automatically with the following data elements:

  • Functional Fit
  • Technical Fit
  • Cost of the relationship between an Application and IT Components

Application Rationalization Survey

You can find the Application Rationalization (TIME) Survey template in the LeanIX Store. Add it to your Workspace and you will be able to use it immediately.

The following information was gathered from stakeholders via Surveys:

  • SAP ERP 6.0 EHP8 (FICO, SD, PP)
    • Technical Performance: 98 percent uptime, but nightly batch windows extend to 10 hours during end-of-month close.
    • Functional Suitability: Handles core billing and inventory valuation, yet lacks real-time analytics and struggles with multi-currency rate updates for U.S. operations.
    • Total Cost of Ownership (TCO): $4 million annual maintenance for on-prem hardware, OS patching, and SAP support.
    • Usage Metrics: 2,500 active users across finance, billing, and network-planning groups.
    • Compliance Considerations: Running SAP ERP 6.0 on an unsupported database version, exposing risk for PCI and telecom regulatory audits.
  • SAP APO 7.13
    • Technical Performance: Several weekly performance bottlenecks when running global demand-planning optimizations.
    • Functional Suitability: Maps to current capacity-planning requirements but cannot handle near-real-time 5G rollout forecasts.
    • TCO: $1.2 million per year on licenses and on-prem maintenance.
    • Usage Metrics: 50 planners run monthly heuristics for a 6- to 12-month horizon; real-time alerts are unavailable.
    • Compliance Considerations: Data staleness risks supply-chain SLA breaches with vendors.
  • Zuora CPQ & Billing
    • Technical Performance: Generally stable, but every patch cycle requires full regression testing of custom rating logic (2-week lead time).
    • Functional Suitability: Supports subscription billing well but does not integrate seamlessly with SAP’s revenue recognition engine.
    • TCO: $800,000 per annum in SaaS fees and custom connector maintenance.
    • Usage Metrics: 3 million invoices generated annually; approximately 15 integration errors per month.
    • Compliance Considerations: Patch delays have caused mismatches in tax calculations, risking regulatory fines.
  • Adobe Experience Manager (AEM) + Adobe Target + Adobe Journey Optimizer
    • Technical Performance: On-prem instances require manual scaling during peak promotions; heaviest traffic during new iPhone launches overwhelms AEM clusters.
    • Functional Suitability: Provides robust content personalization but lacks telecom-specific promotions modules and integrated subscriber analytics.
    • TCO: $1 million annual hosting and licensing; Adobe support level 2 contract.
    • Usage Metrics: 200 marketing campaigns per year; average page load times of 3 seconds under load.
    • Compliance Considerations: Personalization workflows push PII downstream; GDPR/EU privacy controls are manual and inconsistent.
  • Salesforce Service Cloud
    • Technical Performance: Overloaded API calls during major outages, causing latency spikes in case creation for field-service technicians.
    • Functional Suitability: Excellent ticket-routing features, but limited integration into billing workflows—forcing manual reconciliation into SAP ERP.
    • TCO: $2.5 million per year in subscription and integration costs.
    • Usage Metrics: 10,000 open cases per day at peak, with average resolution time of 24 hours.
    • Compliance Considerations: Does not meet Canada’s new telecom data-retention laws without additional customization.
  • Legacy ESB / Custom Middleware
    • Technical Performance: Frequently fails under heavy load; patching for one system often breaks downstream routes (as seen in last year’s 18-hour outage).
    • Functional Suitability: Provides brittle point-to-point mappings rather than a canonical data model; no support for modern APIs or event-driven integration.
    • TCO: $1.8 million in upkeep, specialized middleware operators, and emergency patch cycles.
    • Usage Metrics: Carries roughly 5 million integration messages per day across all systems; 95 percent of messages are synchronous calls that incur high latency.
    • Compliance Considerations: Lacks encryption for in-flight messages; audit logs are patchy, exposing security risk.

By enriching our inventory with these details, we now have a 360-degree view of functional gaps, performance bottlenecks, cost drivers, and compliance concerns across MapleConnect’s top six applications. Save your search results for future use and make use of tags to identify your application in-scope for this rationalization project.

The following is an example of the Provider Cost report:

Other cost reports, such as the Business Capability Cost, give you the cost breakdown by Business Capability based on the underlying cost of Applications and IT Components.

Step 4: Evaluate Data

With enriched data captured, we applied SAP LeanIX’s standard scoring framework, aligned to Gartner’s TIME model, to classify each application’s future. We identified opportunities for rationalization, according to the checklist:

  • Check the Application Landscape Report to understand which Business Capabilities are supported by multiple Applications.

  • Check the Portfolio Report to identify the low-hanging fruits of MapleConnect’s Applications, such as Applications with low Technical and Functional Fit (based on TIME classification).

  • Cluster your Application Landscape by applying TIME to have a clear way forward and a strategy per Application. The following view of the report also shows each application's Functional Fit (left stars) and Technical Fit (right stars).

  • Once you have finalized your analysis, specify the TIME classification using the dedicated field on the Application to define a way forward per Application. LeanIX Calculation features can help automate the TIME classification based on the Technical and Functional Fit values. An out-of-the-box template is available to accelerate the analysis.

The script in the TIME Classification calculation is based on the following matrix:

After saving and activating the calculation, the results can be displayed in the Application Landscape by TIME report.

The resulting classifications:

Application

TIME Classification

Rationale

SAP ERP 6.0 EHP8 (FICO, SD, PP)

Migrate

Functionally core to billing and order-to-cash. Must migrate to SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud to achieve real-time financial analytics and robust compliance.

SAP APO 7.13

Migrate

Supports advanced planning today but lacks real-time forecasting. Transition to SAP IBP on BTP for near-real-time demand planning.

Zuora CPQ & Billing

Eliminate

Legacy performance issues and integration gaps with revenue recognition. Replace with the S/4HANA Private Cloud Billing cockpit to centralize invoicing.

Adobe Experience Manager

Eliminate

High TCO and poor telecom-specific fit. Replace with SAP Sales Cloud + SAP Marketing Cloud for subscriber promotions and personalized offers.

Salesforce Service Cloud

Replace

Best-in-class for support today, but misaligned with “suite-first” mandate. Migrate to SAP Service Cloud on BTP for fully integrated support.

Legacy ESB / Custom Middleware

Eliminate

Single point of failure, patch-driven outages. Replace with SAP BTP Integration Suite for resilient, API-driven, event-based integration.

  • Migrate: Systems that remain functionally critical (SAP ERP 6.0, SAP APO), but require a move into the cloud for real-time capabilities.
  • Replace/Eliminate: Point solutions and middleware that either do not align with our suite-first strategy (Zuora, Adobe AEM, Salesforce) or are too brittle to maintain (legacy ESB).

This evaluation uncovers clear next steps: decommission archaic billing, CRM, and middleware; migrate core planning and finance apps into S/4HANA Cloud and BTP.

Step 5: Create a Roadmap

Armed with the TIME classifications, we crafted a three-year (2025–2028) transformation roadmap. Each wave is aligned to specific business outcomes, clear milestones, and budget estimates (funded by our board’s digital-transformation allocation).

It's worth noting before we start creating the Transformations in the respective Initiatives/Projects, to distinguish the difference between "decommissioning" and "discontinuing" an Application. In SAP LeanIX, "Decommission" and "Discontinue" are distinct transformation types used to manage the lifecycle of applications. Decommission typically refers to the complete removal of an application from the IT landscape, including its data and associated resources, after it's no longer needed. Discontinue, on the other hand, usually means an application is no longer supported or available for new users, but may still exist for a period for existing users or for data retention purposes.

Wave 1 (2025 Q1–Q4): Core Finance & Planning Migration

  • Migrate SAP ERP 6.0 Finance (FICO), PP, and SD to S/4HANA Private Cloud, which means we will introduce S/4HANA Private Cloud and decommission SAP ECC.
    • Extract master data (GL accounts, customers, pricing) and load into S/4HANA Private Cloud sandbox
    • Configure new tax engine, revenue recognition, and fast-close processes
    • Run parallel trials for the month-end close in Q3 2025
  • Migrate SAP APO to SAP IBP (Demand Planning), which means we will introduce SAP IBP and decommission SAP APO, which requires the introduction of SAP BTP first, then SAP IBP, and decommission SAP APO.
    • Stand up IBP planning backbone on BTP
    • Build data feeds from S/4HANA Private Cloud sales forecasts into IBP for near real-time scenario planning
  • Key Deliverables: Migration runbooks, cutover plan for “blackout weekends,” end-user training.

Wave 2 (2026 Q1–Q4): Billing & CRM Rationalization, which means we will roll out SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud Billing and decommission Zuora CPQ & Billing, and introduce SAP Marketing Cloud and SAP Sales Cloud, and decommission legacy applications. Finally, introduce SAP Service Cloud and decommission Salesforce Service Cloud.

  • Eliminate Zuora CPQ & Billing; Rebuild Billing in S/4HANA Private Cloud
    • Recreate subscription billing rules within the S/4HANA Private Cloud Service Billing
    • Migrate historical invoices (past 3 years) via BTP Data Intelligence or Services pipelines
    • Decommission Zuora and retire custom .NET connectors
  • Replace Adobe Experience Manager (AEM) and SAP Customer Relationship Management (CRM) 7.0 EHP 4.0 / Marketing → SAP Emarsys Customer Engagement
    • Rebuild customer self-service portal in SAP Sales Cloud on BTP, leveraging SAP Commerce Service APIs
    • Retire AEM, Adobe Target, and Journey Optimizer; migrate marketing campaigns into SAP Emarsys Customer Engagement
  • Replace Salesforce Service Cloud → SAP Service Cloud
    • Implement SAP Service Cloud for end-to-end ticketing, field-service dispatch, and knowledge management
    • Migrate existing cases and knowledge base; train support agents on the new UI
  • Key Deliverables: Test scripts for billing, marketing, and support; phased go-lives by region.

Wave 3 (2027 Q1–2028 Q2): Integration & Cloud-Native Extensions

  • Decommission Legacy ESB; Adopt SAP BTP Integration Suite
    • Build API catalogue and canonical data model for all core domains (Customers, Subscriptions, Networks)
    • Implement event-based messaging (Enterprise Messaging) for near real-time notifications
    • Migrate 5 million daily integration calls off the ESB onto the BTP Integration Suite
  • Invest in Cloud-Native Microservices & Analytics
    • Develop micro-apps on BTP Extension Factory for specialized network-monitoring dashboards, 5G service provisioning workflows, and advanced subscriber analytics services using SAP Business Data Cloud
    • Retire ad hoc point tools (e.g., Dynatrace, Mirakl) where overlapping functionality exists
  • Key Deliverables: API governance framework, microservice backlogs, and final decommissioning of on-prem middleware.

Each wave is tied to measurable business KPIs, such as faster close cycles, fewer billing errors and improved customer-support SLAs, to track ROI and ensure funding milestones are met. With Cloud ALM integration, these LeanIX initiatives are managed as projects in the Cloud ALM Implementation at a more granular level.

Step 6: Start the Initiative

With the roadmap locked, you can begin the execution immediately:

  • Project Governance Cadence
    • Weekly leadership stand-up (CEO, CIO, VP IT, VP Finance) to review LeanIX dashboards
    • Biweekly “sprint review” with business and IT owners for each wave
  • Wave 1 Kickoff (Q1 2025): Finance & Planning
    • Discovery Workshops: FI/CO teams validate chart-of-accounts mapping, SD order-to-cash flows, and reporting requirements.
    • Data Migration Sprints: Extract GL balances, open AR/AP items, and sales orders from SAP ERP 6.0; cleanse and stage into S/4HANA Cloud sandbox.
    • IBP Integration Proof of Concept: Ingest sample demand data from legacy APO, run what-if scenarios in IBP.
    • Change Management & Training: Conduct “Train-the-Trainer” sessions for finance power users to familiarize them with Fiori-based dashboards and IBP planning workspaces.
  • Wave 2 Planning (Late 2025): Billing & CRM
    • Billing Concept Workshops: Finance, Legal, and Tax teams finalize subscription-billing rules.
    • Portal Design Sprints: UX workshops for Sales & Service Cloud portals; iterate on prototypes using SAP Fiori.
    • Parallel Test Runs: Run five billing cycles in parallel (Zuora and S/4HANA) to validate revenue and tax calculations.

The Initiative Roadmap Report provides a timeline-based visualization of strategic initiatives, showing how they relate to business capabilities, projects, and applications over time. It helps enterprise architects, portfolio managers, and business stakeholders plan, align, and communicate transformation efforts effectively. The following an example of this report showing the three Initiates (Projects) and the Applications impacted.

Throughout all waves, we employ SAP Activate methodology, defining Fit-to-Standard workshops, iterating through configuration sprints, and establishing cutover runbooks that minimize downtime (targeting weekend windows under 12 hours). We also engage a Change-Champion Network (regional IT leads, process ambassadors, and frontline staff) to drive adoption and surface feedback early.

Step 7: Track and Report

Continuous measurement is essential. Using SAP LeanIX Architecture Executive Dashboard, we monitor critical KPIs to our transformation:

  • ERP Transformations KPIs
    • Active Initiatives: applications: Counts the number of initiatives/projects with an active status, which will impact applications. Helps identify which applications are most affected and where efforts may be misaligned with business priorities.
    • Active Initiatives: business capabilities: Assess the number of initiatives/projects with an active status, which will have impact in business capabilities. Helps identify which business capabilities are most affected and where efforts may be misaligned with business priorities.
    • Initiatives Critical for Applications: Measures how many applications are impacted by each initiative, segmented by the initiative’s risk level. It highlights which initiatives are most critical based on both application footprint and associated project risk.
    • Application Modernization (TIME): Measures the percentage of applications classified as "Invest" and "Migrate" based on the Gartner® TIME framework, offering insights to guide the application modernization strategy.
  • Cloud Migration KPI
    • Cloud Migration (by functional fit): Assess applications already in the cloud and use functional fit together with 6R migration strategy framework to monitor cloud transformation progress. Reflects which workloads are suitable for cloud migration, optimization, or decommissioning, supporting targeted, data-driven cloud decisions.
  • Data Quality KPIs
    • Ownership of Applications: Measures the percentage of applications that have subscribers, providing insights into the level of engagement and ownership of fact sheets.
    • Ownership of Business Capabilities: Measures the percentage of business capabilities that have subscribers, providing insights into the level of engagement and ownership of fact sheets.
    • Lifecycle Completion of Applications: Assesses the ratio of existing fact sheets with lifecycle status completed, providing visibility into data completeness and supporting effective portfolio planning and transition management.
  • Application Portfolio Assessment KPIs
    • Functional Fit of Applications: This assesses the degree to which applications align with intended business processes and requirements, based on the percentage of applications classified as "Appropriate" or "Perfect".
    • Application Portfolio (by lifecycle): Assess the number of applications categorized by their lifecycle status, supporting visibility into the health and evolution of the application portfolio, aiding in planning, modernization, and decommissioning efforts.
    • Applications in "end-of-life": Assess applications reaching their end-of-life stage to ensure proactive decision-making about replacements, upgrades, or retirements.
  • Application Rationalization KPI
    • Application Rationalization (TIME): Measures the percentage of applications classified as "Tolerate" and "Eliminate" based on the Gartner® TIME framework, offering insights to guide the application rationalization strategy.

Dashboards in LeanIX automatically roll up application statuses, migration progress, and risk heatmaps. Monthly “Transformation Reviews” with the CIO and CEO ensure executive visibility. Quarterly “Value Realization” reports covering hard savings, operational improvements, and customer-experience gains to keep both business sponsors and regulators apprised.

All SAP LeanIX workspaces come with use case-specific KPI dashboards, including application rationalization. Use one or copy and adjust the dashboard to your organization's specific requirements.

For the Application Rationalization use case, the Application Rationalization Dashboard measures the health of your rationalization initiatives with critical analytics like:

  1. Application Roadmap
  2. Application Landscape - TIME
  3. Cost Analysis TIME Assessment
  4. Optimization Candidates
  5. Data Quality
  6. TIME Assessment
  7. Application Portfolio by TIME and Business Criticality
  8. Functional Fit per Application
  9. Technical Fit per Application

Conclusion

By following this seven-step rationalization framework, grounding strategy, scoping applications, enriching data, evaluating via Gartner TIME, crafting a phased roadmap, executing in waves, and rigorously tracking outcomes, MapleConnect Communications will shift from a brittle, best-of-breed on-premises estate to a unified, cloud-native SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud and BTP platform. The result? More resilient networks, faster billing and customer onboarding, reduced TCO, and a superior subscriber experience enabling MapleConnect to hit its $200 billion revenue goal, slash churn, and secure its leadership position in the North American telecom market.

entry.title: Against all odds – Integrated Supplier on the SAP Business Network ...
entry.content:
Against all odds – OQEMA AG managed to integrate the S/4HANA Cloud, private edition with the SAP Business Network (integration scenario scope item 4A1) and successfully went live with the pilot market

Introduction

OQEMA AG has been an integrated (system-to-system integration) supplier on the SAP Ariba Network (now called SBN) since 2019, leveraging the so-called Business Suite Add-on (BSAO).

It’s no coincidence, that in the same year, DSAG and SAP launched an initiative to jointly develop a successor solution for the BSAO. In September 2019, as part of this initiative, the DSAG submitted a list of BSAO deficiencies/shortcomings to SAP. This list was then compiled and prioritized by the member companies of the DSAG Ariba Working Group (beginning of April 2020) and identified several must-have requirements, out of which one has been the need for "Provide an approval workflow for order inbound changes." It may sound a minor requirement, but it's probably a key must-have for most customers, as a simple change to the order on the customer (buyer) side can lead to inconsistent changes to the sales order on the supplier side with corresponding business disruptions in the downstream process.

An example: The customer orders product A for delivery date X. The sales order is created and processed on the supplier side. Typically, an outbound delivery is created with a certain lead time. From this point on, the delivery date can no longer be changed (the delivery would have to be deleted). However, the customer (buyer) can submit an order change (update) at any time and, for example, adjust the desired delivery date. Due to an integration error, the supplier does not check whether the delivery date can still be changed at all. Instead, the date is "simply" changed. This problem already existed in the previous integration with SAP ECC. However, there was at least the option to detect and prevent this situation using a customer-specific extension (BAdI). The new solution does not offer a corresponding extension option. The idea behind "Provide an approval workflow for order inbound changes" would not only solve this problem but also improve the process, giving both parties visibility in case of changes to the purchase order.

At the end of February 2022, SAP approached us and asked us to create an influence request, which would be required for the implementation of the solution. This influence request, dated March 1, 2022, has now received 43 votes.

Subsequently, the initiative worked together on the design of the solution. In 2024, a user experience test was successfully conducted with several companies using a PoC.

And now? We're now in 2025, and SAP still hasn’t been able to confirm any release date for the solution. We can only speculate about the reasons for this, and yes, they may be diverse. However, one important aspect could be the "chicken and egg" problem. Since 2023, there has been a code split between the SAP S/4HANA public cloud and the on-premises/private cloud.

4A1, the scope item that succeeds BSAO, has been generally available (GA) since the S/4HANA 2020 release, but adoption appears to have been low, especially in the public cloud (although not limited to this environment) - not really surprising, isn’t it?

Without adoption, there's no priority and no development resources – without improvements, there's no adoption. A dilemma!

Our challenge

We at OQEMA AG knew that we would need to replace our existing integration with SAP SBN and say goodbye – both to our ECC and to the BSAO. But how can we achieve this if the “product” has functional gaps and there is no real commitment to solve them?

Our way

We had some support from SAP (huge thanks to those who always supported us and believed in us) and many many creative workarounds on our end. In May 2025 we successfully went live with our pilot scenario. Currently, we have integrated the order-in and order-confirmation process for direct material with SAP S/4HANA, private cloud release 2023 FPS1.

What’s next?

Trying to do the same magic for the advanced shipping notification and invoice integration and paving the way to finally say goodbye to SAP ECC & BSAO!

And you?

We are curious how other suppliers managed to integrate their SAP S/4HANA backend system with the SBN. Are you already on S/4HANA or is your transformation project still ongoing? Who is also using the standard integration that comes with scope item 4A1? Please reach out to team-up and influence SAP’s product roadmap.

entry.title: Re: Code for finding the factorial of a no
entry.content:
CREATE FUNCTION FACTORIAL(IP INTEGER)
RETURNS OP INTEGER
LANGUAGE SQLSCRIPT
READS SQL DATA
AS
BEGIN
                                    DECLARE I INTEGER := 1;
                                    OP := 1;
                                    WHILE I <= :IP
                                    DO
                                    OP := OP * I;
                                    I := I+1;
                                    END WHILE;
END;

 

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Best Regards,

Madhu

entry.title: Custom logic for checking journal entry submitter against approvers
entry.content:
Dear SAP,

We have a business case

Multiple level Approval for Verify Journal Entry - number of workflow are pre-defined with 5 level base on amount

1. Level_1 <5,000 USD

.Approver 1 :  with userid A

.Approver 2 :  with userid B

2. Level_2 : > 5,001 & < 20,000 USD

Approver 1 :  with userid C

Approver 2 :  with userid D

3. Level_3 : > 20,001 & <200,000 USD

Approver 1 :  with userid E

4. Level_4 : > 200,001 & <500,000 USD

Approver 1 :  with userid F

5. Level_5 : > 500,001 USD

Approver 1 :  with userid G

All approvers are stored on this CDS:  I_RESPYMGMTTEAMMEMBERAPI

Journal Entry - Verify by Requester:

  • CDS VIEW name: C_GLJRNLENTRYTOBEVERIFIED
  • CDS field name: PARKEDBYUSER

which custom logic that can perform checking submitter [PARKEDBYUSER] against approver on CDS view I_RESPYMGMTTEAMMEMBERAPI

and if PARKEDBYUSER is found on which level then that approval level is kept and go to next level.

is it this one?

* Business Context: Journal Entry Verification in General Ledger
* BAdI Description: Value evaluation of additional conditions for scenarios

SCENARIO_ID = WS02800046

if yes, please guide the logic for keep the approval level and go next level

Patrickc

entry.title: Re: Issue Importing Customer Master Data with Multiple Co...
entry.content:
Hi

I understand you are considering the option of

- having all cust_sales ( concatanated dimension), customer(without concatenation), sales org, division etc as seperate dimensions and

- Having customer, sales org, division etc as also attributes of cust_sales and

- defining validation between cust_sales and customer/sales org etc via cust_sales attributes.

please see the details of "how to create validation rules based on attributes of another dimension".

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/sap-analytics-cloud-planning-validation-rules/ba-p/13475166

https://learnsap.enable-now.cloud.sap/pub/mmcp/index.html?show=project!PR_12FD79C240A0CCA4:uebung#4 .

If you will go with that design, please check the limits in the same blog / interative learning because in your case there are many validation relevant dimensions and links. I don't think you can have that many properties in the same validation rule ( as the limit I believe was 3 properties - don't have a system to check). As I mentioned before, it may worth checking if this granularity requirement for planning is really inevitable.

entry.title: Re: REBV condition type showing negative value even after...
entry.content:
Hi,

You can consider the following approach:

  1. Review Condition Type Settings: Ensure that the condition type settings are correctly configured to reflect positive values. Double-check the configuration in the condition contract to ensure that the settings are not overridden elsewhere.

  2. Check +/- Sign Change Feature: Utilize the +/- sign change feature in SAP S/4HANA Cloud. Top 5 Things to Know About +/- Sign Change for Group Reporting This feature allows you to reverse the +/- sign for values in reports, which can be useful if the values are stored as negative but need to be displayed as positive. You can manage this feature in the ‘Manage Global Hierarchies’ app.

  3. Adjust Hierarchy Settings: If you are using a hierarchy for consolidation financial statement items, ensure that the +/- sign change is selected for the relevant nodes. This will ensure that the values are displayed correctly in your reports.

Best regards, Dequan Xu

entry.title: SAP MaxAttention IWS 'Accelerate your Financial Processes: SAP Business AI & Beyond' (July 1, 2025)
entry.content:
Virtual

July 1, 2025

Agenda

Slides & Recording

Dear Valued Customer,

In today's fast-paced financial landscape, it is essential for finance departments to prioritize their transformation strategies effectively to overcome challenges and capitalize on opportunities. On July 1, 2025, experts from the Center of Expertise Finance and Industry Solutions EMEA held the SAP MaxAttention Innovation Workshop titled "Accelerate your Financial Processes: SAP Business AI and Beyond." The event featured key insights and innovations that demonstrate how SAP Business AI can streamline operations and enhance decision-making. This blog provides an overview of the topics and highlights presented during the workshop.

Workshop Kick-Off: Exploring the Potential of SAP Business AI

The workshop began with an introduction and a discovery of the potential and background of SAP Business AI in finance.  

Introduction to AI-Powered Capabilities in SAP Finance

Following the introduction, the workshop delved into AI-powered capabilities within SAP Finance. SAP utilizes AI capabilities across its cloud solutions to automate and enhance finance processes, including document processing, recommendations, and forecasting. These technologies open new possibilities in finance applications, allowing finance teams to shift from manual tasks to a more strategic focus.

Finance Use Cases: Intelligent Goods and Invoice Receipt Reconciliation and Cash Application

The workshop then transitioned to specific finance use cases, presenting intelligent Goods and Invoice Receipt Reconciliation, as well as the Cash Application. Participants explored an enhanced Goods and Invoice Receipt Account Reconciliation process, designed to provide a streamlined and accelerated reconciliation experience using new capabilities like Machine Learning. In another session, attendees learned about automating the clearing of incoming payments with SAP Cash Application. This tool employs machine learning to efficiently match electronic bank statement items to open receivables, making the process faster and more accurate. The session highlighted the product’s functionality, value, and real-life examples based on customer experiences and expert knowledge.

Cross-Industry Knowledge Transfer: SAP Billing and Revenue Innovation Management

The workshop also featured a cross-industry knowledge transfer session focused on SAP Billing and Revenue Innovation Management. Customers shared experiences of transforming their IT solution architecture to SAP Billing and Revenue Innovation Management. The session aimed to provide lessons learned from architecture, design, and technical topics, and recommend next steps to mitigate risks.

Workshop Conclusion: Starting Your Journey and SAP Support

The sessions concluded with a recap on how to start the transformation journey, including insights on SAP's support options to kick-start the transformation. These sessions presented various AI finance use cases, provided demos, and included Q&A segments to help customers grasp the SAP product portfolio and innovative technologies in the finance area. We also introduced our AI Service Offerings, highlighting how we can support companies from the SAP MaxAttention side especially in the Finance area.

Now more than ever, it is crucial to assist companies in becoming successful and adapting to changing markets by transforming into intelligent enterprises within the experience economy.

Our Workshops

These sessions and workshop series focus on a specific topic and the purpose is to enable our customers to understand the product portfolio and innovative technologies of SAP in order to drive adoption and consumption. As specifically now, it is more important than ever to help companies to be successful and to respond to changing markets by transforming into intelligent enterprises in the experience economy.

Your Feedback

Feel free to share your feedback and thoughts in the comment section below.

entry.title: Re: LOG OF CHANGES TO USERS IN SAP S4HANA
entry.content:
You changed the end date of validity and couldn't find it in CDPOS.

Look at the related data element:

  • USR02-GLTGB > XUGLTGB. The "Change Document" box isn't checked on the "Further Characteristics" tab.
  • Therefore, the change isn't logged.
  • As far as I know, this was also the case in ECC (checked in Ehp 7).

Did you look at table USH02 - Change history for logon data  ?

entry.title: Jonys Arcanjo
entry.content:
More about Jonys Arcanjo in SAP Community



First Name, Last Name: Jonys Arcanjo
Location (City, Country): Fortaleza, Brazil
Occupation: SAP Consultant – SAP Analytics Cloud | ML and Generative AI

 


Jonys, tell us about yourself.
Who are you, what do you like to do, what is important to you?

I’m an SAP consultant passionate about data-driven decision-making, with over 12 years of experience helping companies turn information into action through technologies such as SAP Analytics Cloud, Data Intelligence, Machine Learning, and Generative AI. I live in Fortaleza, in the northeast of Brazil, and I believe in the power of communities as catalysts for growth.

Outside of work, I enjoy sharing knowledge, organizing SAP community events, and exploring ways to apply technology to solve real-world problems. What I value most is contributing to the growth of those around me and creating genuine connections through knowledge.


What is your area of expertise?
What does a usual day at work look like for you?

My expertise lies in analytical solutions, with a focus on SAP Analytics Cloud, Data Intelligence, and AI-driven initiatives. On a typical day, I work on end-to-end solution development—from data integration to visualization and insight generation.

I’m also involved in automation and process optimization projects, always aligning technology with business needs. A constant in my work is collaboration with different teams, always aiming to deliver real value to the client.


What inspired you to become an SAP Champion in SAP Community?

The SAP Community has always played a key role in my journey. Since the beginning of my career, I’ve found support, inspiration, and learning there.

Becoming an SAP Champion is a way to give back everything I’ve received from the community: I want to encourage other professionals, especially from Latin America, to share their experiences and realize the impact they can make by connecting with people around the world.


How do you learn in SAP Community?
What is your daily/weekly routine in SAP Community?

I have a habit of following blogs and Q&A sessions weekly, especially in the areas of SAP Analytics Cloud and Data Intelligence. I also enjoy writing content and sharing lessons learned from day-to-day challenges.

Whenever possible, I join discussions and local events like SAP Inside Track Fortaleza, which I’ve helped organize for several years. For me, the constant exchange of experiences is what makes the community so rich.


What is your suggestion to get the most out of SAP Community?

Be consistent and intentional. Follow tags related to the topics you enjoy most, engage with content (even if you have simple questions), and share what you’ve learned—even if you think it’s basic. The Community thrives on diverse experiences, and every contribution matters. Also, participating in local or virtual events is a great way to expand your network and spark new ideas.

A question from Jonys to you:
“What was the most defining turning point in your SAP journey, and how did the SAP Community support you at that moment?”

entry.title: Missing possibility to filter the language in the infoobject text maintenance
entry.content:
Recently we updated our BW to BW2023 in our company. I like using the web cockpit in BW4 as it is the pushed UI to use for managing infobjects and infoproviders. But one thing is really bugging me and even in the newest version of BW it is not possible: Filtering the language of texts in the master data maintenance is not possible. It seems like such a basic feature. Why is language not a standard filter or at least available as an additional filter?
If you have a customer owned Infoobject and are maintaining texts via web cockpit, it would be very useful to filter language. This would make it very easy to spot missing translations in a specifc language or to copy/paste texts of a specific language.

Is this a customizing issue in our system or just a missing feature? I'd put a request in the customer influence plattform, but unfortunately there is none for SAP BW.

entry.title: SAC/Seamless Planning – Transactions Recorded at Higher Hierarchy Nodes
entry.content:
Hello everyone,

I’ve created a planning model in SAC and successfully loaded the required master data from DSP through Seamless Planning. One dimension in the model is hierarchical.
When importing the transaction data, I noticed that some source entries are recorded only at higher-level hierarchy nodes (e.g., cost center) and not at the leaf level (e.g., individual postings). Consequently, these records are not imported into my model.

Have any of you encountered this issue before? How did you handle scenarios where data isn’t booked at the leaf level but needs to be correctly imported into the planning model?
